summary: Joel Spolsky discusses Fog Creek's need for more office space, sharing the formulas and considerations involved in calculating office space requirements. He breaks down square footage allocations per employee and common areas, explains the difference between carpetable and rentable square footage, and humorously critiques landlord practices around loss factors. He also explores strategies for handling growth during long-term leases, including a novel idea of subletting excess space to software startups as premium programmer-friendly offices.
key_points: Individual offices of 70-100 sqft per employee plus 30% circulation overhead form the basis of office space planning
Rentable square footage advertised by landlords includes significant unusable space like elevator shafts, mechanicals, and common areas, making it misleading for tenants
Growing companies face a tension between long-term leases (5-10 years) and unpredictable space needs, with no perfect solution
Fog Creek considered an early version of coworking: subletting excess space to bootstrapping startups in programmer-optimized offices at ~$1500/month
money_quotes: It would be really nice if office space listings were given in carpetable square feet, because that's what the tenant cares about.
The building owner's house in Queens which if you tried to go near a goon would shoot you in the knees (despite his very fetching daughter trying to seduce you from her bedroom window).

In reality, this just gets you in the ballpark, and every space is different (long narrow spaces need more circulation space) so there's no alternative to having your architect take the floor plans and actually figure out how many offices will fit in there.

Getting too much space and renting it out in small chunks to software startups that need an office or two while they're bootstrapping, and who would appreciate a working environment designed for programmers rather than a coffee shop.
The trouble is that it would end up costing something on the order of $1500 a month for a private, Fog Creek-quality office, which might be out of the range of bootstrapped startups.
key_insight: Office space planning for growing software companies is a messy optimization problem where landlord metrics obscure reality and long-term leases conflict with unpredictable growth.
